title: The Evangelion of Books
wikitext:
[]: The Evangelion of Books             Quote Post by Gorbatschow » Wed Jan 15, 2014 1:58 pm I'm not sure If there is already a thread like this one, but I couldn't find one. If this thread is a dublicate, feel free to bag it and tag it. I'm searching for a book that can be declared "The Evangelion of Books", in other words: I'm searching for a book that is: * Multilayered * Elaborated * Deals with psychological, sociological or philosophical topics * Has a lot of subtext that is embedded in a good, stylish "Device-plot". (The whole NERV-Plot is just a"device" to commence the character-based and psychological layers, but still really sophisticated and intelligent.) I hope anyone can suggest me a book, since I already have favorite romances, criminals, tragedies and so on, but no favorite "complex, interpretable, thinking-book" -Sincere regards.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Wed Jan 15, 2014 2:51 pm The bible? :/ Serious answer: 2001, a Space Odyssey, and more specifically, the Space Odyssey series. I haven't read it myself, but there is the direct creative influence, and the similarity in how people used to discuss it in the same way we discuss Eva.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Jan 15, 2014 2:55 pm The only thing that I can think of that comes at all close would be Peake's Gormenghast Trilogy, or at least the first two volumes. The Eva Monkey wrote: The bible? :/

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Jan 15, 2014 3:00 pm Lots of sex and violence, though. Suggestions: The Count of Monte Cristo The Second Apocalypse Series A Song of Ice and Fire House of Leaves It

[]:             Quote Post by Chainsaw Owl » Wed Jan 15, 2014 4:18 pm Frankenstein : Man creates a monster in his own image, using the body of a mysterious donor(s). In the end, man is unable to cope with his monster's destructive potential, as well as its potential to create. Seriously though, I've been looking for a book that tickles my Evangelion nerve for years. Truth be told, I haven't exactly been looking in the right genre, but I'm sure there's something out there that wasn't just written by an overly obsessed fan. I've heard others sing the praises of Gormenghast as well, which causes me to suspect that it might actually be worth a read. I'll keep an eye out on my next foray to ye olde bookstore.

[]:             Quote Post by Joy Evangelion » Wed Jan 15, 2014 4:19 pm Infinite Jest by David Foster Wallace. Reminds me of Eva for so many reasons, but I won't spoil anything. Full of great characters, deals with depression, super multilayered(there's like 100 pages of endnotes), Wallace's prose is very stylish, the book sucks you in, and it came out in 1996.

[]:             Quote Post by Gorbatschow » Wed Jan 15, 2014 4:21 pm Chuckman wrote: A Song of Ice and Fire House of Leaves It

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Wed Jan 15, 2014 4:40 pm The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Wed Jan 15, 2014 4:42 pm I wonder what is the Evangelion of annoying recommendation threads Gorba keeps creating. Also TEM just fucked you over by recommending you "2001" books over the film. Film is superior and the book sequels are awful.

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Wed Jan 15, 2014 4:43 pm C.A.P. wrote: The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Jan 15, 2014 4:44 pm The Left Behind series.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Jan 15, 2014 5:07 pm To be fair, the 2001 novel is basically a novelization of the movie which itself loosely based on Clarke's short "The Sentinel".

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Jan 15, 2014 5:15 pm The 2001 novel is to the movie as the Eva manga is to the series. Make of that what you will.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Jan 15, 2014 5:26 pm Mr. Tines wrote: Somewhat lacking on the "plot" thing, what with being an anthology and all that.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Jan 15, 2014 5:35 pm Bible 2 sucked, MC was a total Gary Stu who even got an asspull resurrection for no reason. OG Biblers represent!!

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Wed Jan 15, 2014 5:57 pm I'm tempted to nominate Murakami's Kafka on the Shore here. Chainsaw Owl wrote: I've heard others sing the praises of Gormenghast as well, which causes me to suspect that it might actually be worth a read. I'll keep an eye out on my next foray to ye olde bookstore.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Jan 15, 2014 7:30 pm Generic answer: Watchmen (not really though)

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Jan 15, 2014 7:32 pm Watchmen and Eva have so many similarities it hurts. They're even generally about some of the same things.

[]:             Quote Post by Guy Nacks » Wed Jan 15, 2014 7:46 pm Without saying Watchmen , which was my first answer, I'd go with Philip Pullman's His Dark Materials trilogy.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Thu Jan 16, 2014 4:07 am The Harry Potter saga.
